# Regular jump — coaching knowledge

## Phases and correct technique

### Approach
Roll in at a steady, committed pace matched to the jump; do not brake or hesitate at the last second. Cranks level, seat low, weight centered and evenly split between hands and feet. Speed should be enough to clear, no more — momentum-only clearing is risky.
- **Visual cues:** Rider standing in neutral "attack" stance — arms and knees slightly bent, back fairly flat, head up looking ahead down the trail.
- Pedals level (horizontal cranks), not mid-pedal-stroke.
- Body centered over the bottom bracket, bike tracking straight at the takeoff.

### Compression / loading
Just before the base of the lip, dip/coil down through arms and legs like a spring to pre-load. On full-suspension, drive the bike down to squash it into the takeoff. The more pop wanted, the deeper the dip. Do NOT stay stiff, and do NOT over-bend knees (loses leg power).
- **Visual cues:** Rider lowest point — hips dropped, elbows and knees flexed, bike compressed into the transition.
- Weight still centered, not shifted rearward.
- Chest over bars, eyes up.

### Takeoff / extension
Drive body weight UP with the legs, extending smoothly so arms and legs reach full extension right as the front wheel leaves the lip. Front wheel lifts first, then the rear wheel is popped/scooped using the full length of the takeoff — never both wheels at once. Resist softening the arms (that dumps weight forward). Let the takeoff rotate you slightly back; keep head in line with the bars. Point toes down / add slight back-pedal pressure to keep feet planted.
- **Visual cues:** Body extended and tall, standing roughly perpendicular to the ground (not leaning back or forward).
- Front wheel already rising while rear tire is still using the last of the lip.
- Arms nearly straight and under tension pulling lightly on the bars, head aligned over the handlebar.
- Steeper lip → rider more forward over the front; mellow lip → rider more upright and further back from bars.

### Air
Once off, let the bike level beneath you and stay relaxed. Bring the rear wheel up by curling feet and lifting with the hips, not by yanking the pedals. Arms bend to draw the bars toward the hips for front-wheel control, then knees flex to adjust pitch. Spot the landing early.
- **Visual cues:** Bike level or matching trajectory, both wheels roughly parallel to the ground/landing.
- Rider centered over the bike, mass over the front-wheel contact patch, limbs relaxed and symmetrical (even left/right).
- Eyes fixed on the landing, body balanced — no flailing limbs.

### Landing
Match the bike's angle to the landing slope and touch down with both wheels together (or very slightly front-wheel first) high on the downslope, not at the flat bottom. Use arms and legs as suspension to absorb impact; hardtails need more leg extension/absorption range.
- **Visual cues:** Bike pitch mirrors the landing ramp angle on approach to touchdown.
- Limbs slightly bent and ready to compress, NOT locked dead-straight.
- Wheels landing together on the transition, rider centered and looking ahead.

## Common mistakes

### Dead sailor (deceased mariner)
- **Signature:** Rider tensed and rigid mid-air, arms/legs stiff and splayed, often drifting to one side or going squint; frozen posture.
- **Cause:** Panic in the air, stiffening up; pulling up hard with the arms instead of driving weight up with the legs; weight too far back over the rear axle; favoring a dominant left/right side pull.
- **Consequence:** Loss of balance, sideways/off-camber landing, crash.
- **Fix:** "Slow the whole motion down, let the lip do the work, then chill and relax — you're just along for the flight. Pull evenly 50/50 on the bars and land two wheels."

### Nose dive / Ronnie Mac air (pitching forward)
- **Signature:** Front wheel drops in the air, bike pitched nose-down; front wheel lands first and heavy.
- **Cause:** Center of gravity ahead of the force driving the rear wheel; softening/absorbing with the arms on the takeoff; not shifting weight back enough before popping; steep/sharp kicker decelerating the bike so the front drops. Also caused by tensing up and doing nothing off the lip.
- **Consequence:** OTB, hard front-wheel impact.
- **Fix:** "Create upward momentum with your body to counter the bike's forward rotation. Allow the takeoff to rotate you back, stay relaxed, and pop through the legs."

### Arms-first extension / bucking
- **Signature:** Aggressive yank on the bars, front end whipping up violently; on too much speed the rider gets kicked/bucked over the front.
- **Cause:** Pulling the front up with arms rather than driving legs; hitting the takeoff too hard/too fast.
- **Consequence:** Loss of control, dead sailor, or getting bucked OTB.
- **Fix:** "It shouldn't feel like an aggressive pull — legs drive everything, arms and front end are just along for the ride. Put the extra energy into the preload squash, not the bars."

### Weight too far back
- **Signature:** Rider's hips behind the saddle/rear axle at takeoff.
- **Cause:** Getting scared and shifting back.
- **Consequence:** Dramatically increases dead-sailor likelihood and loss of front-wheel control.
- **Fix:** "Keep your weight at least centered over the bike — aim about 70% up / 30% forward. Weight should feel slightly heavier in your feet than your hands."

### Going over the bars (over-compression)
- **Signature:** Rider catapulted forward off the front; body weight loading the front wheel on takeoff.
- **Cause:** Preloading/compressing too much with weight all over the back, then springing forward.
- **Consequence:** OTB crash.
- **Fix:** "Keep weight central — don't bury it over the back. Preload smoothly, don't overcook the squash."

### Spud hop (rear wheel up too early / clip reliance)
- **Signature:** Rear wheel lifts a full bike-length before the lip; clipped-in riders yanking the bike up with their feet.
- **Cause:** Not using the whole transition; relying on clipless pedals to manipulate the bike.
- **Consequence:** Uneven pop between wheels, weird air, weight thrown forward, wasted takeoff.
- **Fix:** "Use the full takeoff — front wheel off the lip, pause, then pop the rear. Rely on technique, not your cleats. Curl the feet, lift with the hips."

### Fake floater / pulling up instead of pumping
- **Signature:** Bike pulled up and tucked underneath the rider for hang time; rider always needs more speed to clear.
- **Cause:** Yanking the bike off the ground rather than generating true pop off the lip.
- **Consequence:** Very inefficient, robs distance and control.
- **Fix:** "You don't jump on your feet by yanking them off the ground — pump the bike into the takeoff and let the lip launch you."

### The crumple (stiff/folding on big jumps)
- **Signature:** Rider folds into the bike like a deck chair under G-force, then gets spat into the air limp.
- **Cause:** Defaulting to the loose "absorb everything" tactic on a big jump; too passive against the G-forces.
- **Consequence:** Uncontrolled launch, near-misses, crashes.
- **Fix:** "On bigger jumps you must stand up to the jump and push back against the G-force — resist, don't collapse."

### Stiff / dead-straight landing
- **Signature:** Arms and legs locked out straight at touchdown.
- **Cause:** Not preparing to absorb.
- **Consequence:** Arms can fold the wrong way, hands/feet blown off, back landing.
- **Fix:** "Land in the attack position, limbs bent, use your body as extra suspension."

### Taking off crooked / (beep) air
- **Signature:** Bike veers sideways/squint immediately after leaving the lip.
- **Cause:** Uneven left-right hand pressure up the takeoff inducing a small steering input.
- **Consequence:** Off-balance flight, sketchy landing.
- **Fix:** "Press evenly through both hands up the face; keep your mass centered over the front wheel so you can steer out of it on landing."

### Wrong speed
- **Signature (too slow):** Comes up short, cases the landing/knuckle, wheels stay level and flat in the air. **(too fast):** Overshoots to flat, gets bucked.
- **Cause:** Bad speed judgment; using speed to substitute for technique.
- **Consequence:** Casing, OTB, overshoot crashes.
- **Fix:** "Find a steady pace. Judge speed by following a rider in or watching others. Build height with pop on small jumps rather than just adding speed."
- **Disagreement:** Most coaches teach active pop/lift for height; one emphasizes for beginners doing *nothing* but staying unweighted with correct speed off the lip is the safest first step.

## Frame-selection guidance
- **Base-of-lip / deepest compression:** Best frame to judge loading — look for coiled limbs, centered weight, level pedals. Reveals over-compression or stiffness.
- **Front wheel leaving the lip:** Key extension frame — should show near-full arm/leg extension, front wheel rising, rear still on the takeoff. Reveals arms-first bucking, weight-back, or early rear-wheel lift.
- **Rear wheel leaving the lip:** Confirms whether the full takeoff was used and pop direction; back should be near perpendicular to flat ground.
- **Peak of arc:** Best frame to judge air control and pitch — bike level, rider centered and relaxed, both wheels parallel. Reveals dead sailor, nose dive, crooked/squint air.
- **Just before touchdown:** Judges landing setup — bike pitch matching landing angle, both wheels aligned, limbs bent to absorb. Reveals nose-dive, dead-straight limbs, wrong landing point (knuckle vs. downslope).
- Compare front-vs-rear wheel timing across two adjacent frames to spot spud hops and simultaneous two-wheel takeoffs.

## Coaching voice
- Keep it encouraging and confidence-first — jumping is "the funnest thing on a bike"; frame progress as achievable and fun, not scary.
- Lead with safety framing: respect jumps, know how to abort/roll, start small and work up even when experienced.
- Emphasize staying relaxed, loose, and committed over forcing technique — "tell your bike what to do."
- Explain the *why* (physics, forces, bike being a fraction of body weight) so riders understand, not just memorize.
- Give one clear cue at a time in plain rider language ("stand up to the jump," "let the lip do the work," "land two wheels").
- Stress progression: master timing/pop on small mellow tabletops before size, speed, or gaps.
- Normalize mistakes — "I still do these" — to reduce fear and pressure.
- Reassure that even good jumpers can't fully explain it; practice and reps matter more than perfect theory.
